data.title: Ditch Digital Realty For Equinix
data.description:
Equinix (EQIX) and Digital Realty (DLR) combine to form just about the entirety of the REIT data center universe having gobbled up most of their peers. The largest remaining publicly traded peer is Iron Mountain (IRM) which only gets a small portion of its revenue from data centers.

So, if a REIT investor wants liquid exposure to data centers, they largely have to choose between EQIX and DLR. This article will discuss which is the better investment and why.

EQIX and DLR are ostensibly similar companies. They are both large caps at $71B and $55B, respectively. They are highly global operations with nearly identical property maps overlaying the major data center hubs around the world. (We've written about both EQIX before here and DLR here.)

Yet I posit that Equinix is a better investment with a better track record, operations and valuation.

Track record

Equinix has had far superior AFFO/share growth to DLR.

We are using 1Q13 as the start date of comparison because that is the earliest we were able to reliably source AFFO data for both companies. Equinix started thinking about becoming a REIT in 2012 and started reporting AFFO routinely.

Since the first quarter of 2013, Equinix has more than tripled its AFFO/share while DLR has not even doubled.

This is not a quarter specific anomaly, but rather EQIX consistently growing at a faster pace for the entire period. Below we charted the AFFO/share of EQIX and DLR for each quarter.

Digital Realty did okay, but Equinix trounced them.

I believe much of the difference came down to operations and strategy.

Equinix built ecosystems while DLR built commodities

Both companies own high quality top-of-the-line data centers, but even top quality is a commodity because once the blueprints are out, anyone with enough capital can just build their own.

The largest tenants of data centers are the hyperscalers like Microsoft, Meta, Google, Amazon, et cetera. These companies obviously have the capital to build their own data centers. They do build some of their own and they also rent some space from EQIX and DLR.

Digital Realty saw opportunity in building commodity data centers because they could do so at very high ROIC. Many new data center builds could be executed at mid-teens IRRs and leased to hyperscalers. Upon delivery, these were immediately accretive to AFFO/share.

Equinix also builds lots of data centers at similarly high IRRs. The difference is that EQIX seemed to focus more or at least had more success in cross-connects.

Direct connections between tenants make a data center more than just the commodity of the physical building. They become an ecosystem in which the ability to connect to tenants already present in a data center worked as a positive network externality. It made cabinets in a data center more valuable to new tenants because they didn’t just get power and cooling, but rather access to a variety of direct connections.

Cross connects provide incremental revenue, but the bigger impact, in my opinion, is what they do for negotiating power upon lease expiry.

A hyperscaler tenant that is only interested in power and cooling sees lease renewal as a purely financial transaction. They can build their own data center and thus only want to renew a lease if it is at the right price point. With this threat of switching, they bullied Digital Realty a bit into actually lowering rents on renewal. This manifested negative same-store NOI growth for DLR from 2018-2022.

I was unable to source every period of same-store NOI for EQIX, but I think there are enough data points below to see the difference from DLR.

In that 2018-2022 period where DLR had negative same-store NOI, EQIX was solidly in the positive.

I believe the cross connect ecosystems that EQIX built were a big contributor to their outperformance. A tenant cannot credibly threaten to leave when they are cross-connected to many other tenants and benefit from the reduced latency.

With its impressive growth track record, EQIX has often traded at AFFO multiples in the 30s and typically traded at a premium to DLR. That, in my opinion, was proper pricing as high quality and growth should trade at a high multiple. Indeed, investors who bought Equinix long ago were well rewarded despite the premium because it grew into its price.

In recent months, the valuation has shifted materially in a way that I think makes EQIX an intriguing opportunity.

Valuation.

The crux of the newfound value in EQIX consists of 2 key data points.

  1. EQIX stock price is down about 25%.
  1. AFFO/share has risen and is projected to continue to grow

The consensus (average) of Wall Street analysts following Equinix anticipate AFFO growing to $48.46 in 2028.

With higher AFFO and a lower stock price, EQIX’s multiple has dropped from the 30s to 18.42X

Digital Realty has also gotten a bit cheaper but its price has not fallen as much, and its AFFO has not grown as much. It is trading at 22X forward AFFO.

Looking at the leverage neutral valuation we charted EV/EBITDA for each company below. Equinix, in green, traded at a premium to DLR (yellow) for much of the period but in the last few quarters a gap has opened up with EQIX trading substantially cheaper.

The valuation spread can also be seen in net asset value (NAV).

EQIX is trading at 86.36% of net asset value which is rather unusual for data centers as the sector has often traded above NAV. Even today, DLR is trading at 108.8% of NAV.

Dropping from 30X AFFO to 18X AFFO is a massive multiple compression. It behooves investors to make sure there isn’t a fundamental reason behind the drop.

Forward fundamental outlook

Equinix’s most recent earnings report (3Q25) showed strong growth.

“AFFO of $965 million, an 11% increase over the same quarter of the previous year on an as-reported basis, or a 12% increase on a normalized and constant currency basis driven by strong operating performance and successful balance sheet management resulting in lower net interest expense. $9.83 per share, a 9% increase over the same quarter of the previous year on both an as-reported and a normalized and constant currency basis.”

It seems to have come from broadly healthy operations including record bookings and growing MRR:

  • “Record annualized gross bookings of $394 million, up 25% year over year and 14% over Q2 2025
  • Monthly recurring revenue (MRR) increased 8% on both an as-reported and a normalized and constant currency basis over the same quarter last year”

Along with earnings, EQIX increased guidance.

Thus, there was nothing concerning in the operations to date. Perhaps the market is sensing a negative inflection going forward.

In my opinion, EQIX remains on a strong growth trajectory. 3 aspects of operations point to healthy AFFO growth:

  1. Stable to slightly growing occupancy at existing data centers.
  2. Large pipeline of development/expansion.
  3. High ROIC on capital spent.

Utilization has been stable for a long time. Per the 10-K:

“Our cabinet utilization rates were approximately 78% and 80%, as of September 30, 2024 and 2023, respectively.”

Thus, I suspect existing properties will provide a steady base of AFFO. AFFO growth will likely come from a large pipeline at high cap rates.

EQIX has $1.5B in preconstruction.

$2.9B in construction.

And another $1.7B in expansion capex.

These investments are at exceedingly high cap rates. Keith Taylor (CFO) discussed rates of return on the 3Q25 earnings call:

“stabilized data centers generate a 26% cash-on-cash return on the gross PP&E invested.”

In REITs spreads of 200 basis points are typical. Equinix has access to debt around 4% so its spread is just astronomical compared to most real estate.

That is how EQIX has grown AFFO so fast historically, and given the pipeline, they seem to still have a long runway of growth.

The bottom line

Data centers are a cornerstone of the AI buildout. Both DLR and EQIX are positioned to benefit, but I think EQIX will outperform due to cheaper valuation and superior operations.

data.title: GameStop's Turnaround Is Working
data.description:
My thesis for investing in GameStop (GME) is that its pivot into collectibles succeeded. This store was originally a place where you could trade in video games, and it still is. But now, it’s also selling sports trading cards and Pokemon cards. While its core gaming business may be shrinking, its collectibles business makes up for that to some extent. GameStop also improved its margins significantly by closing some stores and selling other stores. But it also looks like these improvements might be priced in already.

GameStop just reported its third-quarter 2025 results. Its revenue fell 4.6% from $860.3 million in 3Q 2024 to $821.0 million in 3Q 2025, but its adjusted operating income rose from -$24.6 million in 3Q 2024 to $52.1 million over the same period. By reducing its store count, the company greatly reduced its SG&A expenses. Its efficiency improvements will deliver income growth even if its sales are choppy in upcoming quarters. And that looks likely, because GameStop posted 21.8% quarter-over-quarter revenue growth in the second quarter.

This company is well known for being a meme stock. Its management has also done things that could be considered gimmicks, such as purchasing large amounts of Bitcoin. These factors could affect GameStop’s stock price, so investors should consider them. But I also think it’s worth it to analyze GameStop like other stores that have posted major bottom-line improvements. Other companies in this situation have paid down debt and bought back stock, and these actions have generated EPS growth for companies without much revenue growth. So, the first thing I’d consider is GameStop’s current balance sheet.

GameStop Still Has a Lot of Cash

In the third quarter, GameStop reported $7.84 billion in cash and $10.55 billion in total assets. It has $4.16 billion in long-term debt, so it could easily pay off its debt if management decided to do that. It’s unusual for a store to keep this much cash on its balance sheet because that could make it a hostile takeover target. A fund that’s going for a hostile takeover can use the company’s cash to finance the acquisition. According to Seeking Alpha, GameStop also has TTM free cash flow of $187.8 million, so its cash flow could attract a buyer as well.

Of course, many Wall Street investors are more interested in shorting GameStop, and that’s probably why this company still has so much cash on its balance sheet. If short sellers aggressively target GameStop again, the company has a lot of money that it can use for stock buybacks. Keeping cash in reserve if this happens again might not be the best use of cash for most companies, but it does appear that short sellers are still targeting GameStop.

GameStop’s Pivot Might Catch Short Sellers Off Guard

GameStop currently has 15.4% short interest, according to Seeking Alpha. This company currently has no coverage from Wall Street analysts, so it’s not entirely clear why short sellers are targeting it right now. But its core business, video game trading, appears to be in decline. In the past, many shoppers went to brick-and-mortar stores to buy new video games and consoles, but now many of these purchases take place online on platforms like Steam.

GameStop’s management is well aware of this trend and has pivoted the company into the collectibles market, and this appears to be working. The company’s revenue from collectibles increased from 17.6% in the first 3 quarters of 2024 to 27.5% in the first 3 quarters of 2025. And in the third quarter of 2025 specifically, 31.2% of GameStop’s revenue came from collectibles. So, this company’s collectibles business is rapidly growing and is stabilizing its revenue.

GameStop’s Bitcoin Portfolio Is a Gimmick That Could Pay Off

In its third-quarter report, GameStop reported that it had $519.4 million in digital assets. This balance sheet line refers to its Bitcoin portfolio. Earlier this year, it reported that it bought 4,710 Bitcoin. At a market price of $92,400 per coin on December 10, GameStop’s coins would be only worth $435.2 million. So, GameStop is reporting the value of its digital assets using a longer-term average, and its Bitcoin portfolio recently dropped in value.

But GameStop’s Bitcoin holdings only make up about 5% of its total assets. The company also has positive free cash flow, so it doesn’t have any immediate need to sell its coins. I have no idea what the price of Bitcoin will be in 2026, but if the price goes up, GameStop shareholders will benefit. And if it goes down, this company will still have a strong balance sheet because of its huge cash reserve. I wouldn’t buy GameStop because of its Bitcoin portfolio, but that’s not a reason to sell this stock either. Its Bitcoin investment seems like a gimmick to me, but a potential Bitcoin rally is also an upside risk for short sellers.

GameStop Is Reducing Costs By Closing Stores

GameStop has been reducing its operating costs by selling and closing many of its stores. In its 10-Q filing, it reported that it sold its Canadian subsidiary and its French stores in 2025. It’s also been closing stores in the US. The company’s falling store count explains why its SG&A costs are falling and it partially explains why its revenue is falling as well. The new GameStop is a more efficient company and will have stronger margins in the future. It would be more appropriate to use its current margins, rather than its historical performance, to project its 2026 results. But in a situation like this, where a company is selling stores and closing them down, I’d also project that its revenue will fall as well.

GameStop’s Warrants Are Unlikely to Be Exercised

The 10-Q filing also says that in October 2025, GameStop issued warrants to both its current shareholders and its note holders. These warrants allow their holders to buy GameStop’s stock at a price of $32 per share, and the company could issue as many as 59 million new shares this way. The warrants also expire in October 2026. But GameStop’s stock price at the close on December 10 was $22.12 per share. So, if a huge rally doesn’t occur, it’s likely that many of these warrants won’t be exercised at all.

The Main Risk Is That GameStop’s Pivot Will Fail

Again, GameStop is becoming a store where customers can trade collectibles instead of just a video game store. Its short-term results indicate that this strategy is working, but the company might be benefiting from fads. In the 1990s, there were several collectibles fads, such as Beanie Babies, Tickle Me Elmo Dolls, and Furbies. It’s possible that GameStop’s collectibles won’t retain their value in the future, especially if manufacturers overproduce them.

Comparative Valuation for GameStop

It’s hard to identify direct competitors for GameStop, so I’ll compare it to Build-A-Bear (BBW) and Best Buy (BBY). There’s also no analyst coverage for GameStop, so I can’t use forward EV/EBITDA here like I do with other stocks. But the EV/sales and non-GAAP P/E ratios for GameStop are available, so I’ll use those instead. According to SA, GameStop had a EV/sales ratio of 1.46 after the market close on December 10 and a forward P/E of 23.34. On the same date, Best Buy had a forward EV/sales ratio of 0.44 and a forward non-GAAP P/E of 11.54. And Build-a-Bear had a forward EV/sales ratio of 1.43 and a forward non-GAAP P/E of 13.04.

While GameStop’s margins are quickly improving, it does appear to be trading at a premium compared to the most relevant companies I could use for a comparison.

Valuation for GameStop

GameStop’s revenue fell 4.6% this quarter and its second-quarter result may have been an outlier. So, I’ll project that its 4Q 2025 revenue will drop 4.6% from $1.283 billion to $1.224 billion. GameStop’s revenue for the first 3 quarters of 2025 was $2.523 billion, so its full-year 2025 revenue would then fall 2% to $3.747 billion.

It’s trickier to estimate GameStop’s net income because this company’s net income is higher than its operating income. It’s earning additional interest income and it also owns a Bitcoin portfolio that varies in value. In 3Q 2025, it reported operating income of $41.3 million and adjusted operating income of $52.1 million, so I can estimate that its adjusted net income would also be about $10 million more than the GAAP number on the balance sheet. The company reported $10.7 million in asset impairments that reduced its operating income. So, if I use GameStop’s 3Q GAAP revenue of $821 million and adjust its net income upward by $10 million, it would have $87.1 million in net income and a net margin of 10.6%.

Then, for 2026, I’ll project that GameStop’s revenue will fall another 2% to $3.672 billion while it maintains a 10.6% net profit margin for the full year. The company’s net income for the year would be $389 million. It’s trading at a premium compared to peers, so I’ll project that its P/E will drop to 22 in 2026. So, my estimated market cap would be $8.563 billion.

GameStop’s third-quarter 10-Q filing says it currently has 448 million shares outstanding. So, if I divide a market cap of $8.563 billion by 448 million shares, I get a one-year target price of $19.11 per share. GameStop’s price after the close on December 10 was $22.12, so my estimate projects 14% downside. This scenario is based on what I think is most likely to happen in 2026.

I could also consider a more optimistic scenario where GameStop’s collectible business performs very well in 2026. In this scenario, I’ll project that its revenue will rise 10% from $3.747 billion in 2025 to $4.122 billion in 2026. With a 10.6% net margin, its net income would then be $437 million. And with a 22 P/E, its market cap would be $9.614 billion. If I divide a $9.614 billion market cap by 448 million shares, my one-year target price would then be $21.46 per share. That’s 3% lower than GameStop’s market price of $22.12.

The most recent articles from other SA analysts say GameStop is a hold, and my analysis indicates that it’s a hold as well. This company’s bottom-line performance is likely to improve significantly in 2026 because of its reduced store count, but it looks like this factor may be priced in already. And again, it would be risky to short this stock because it has nearly $8 billion in cash that it could spend on buybacks. But I also expect GameStop’s P/E to fall because it’s higher than peers’ P/E ratios.

Conclusion

GameStop has become a much more efficient company by selling off some of its international stores and closing some of its stores in the US. It also appears that the company’s pivot from video game sales to collectible sales is succeeding as well. So, this appears to be a successful turnaround. And GameStop, unlike other companies, has a lot of cash that it can use for buybacks if short sellers go after it aggressively again. This would be a risky company to short, but other stores might offer better opportunities for longs. So, I’d say GameStop is a hold.

data.title: 2026 Outlook: Why The S&P 500 Is Set To Climb Past 8,000
data.description:
Introduction

The YTD performance of the three key U.S. indices is shown above. As you can see, the Nasdaq (COMP:IND) has advanced the most at 21%, the S&P 500 (SP500) saw a very respectable gain of 17%, and while the Dow Jones (DJI) lagged slightly with a 14% increase, 2025 was still overall a good year for the bulls.

It shouldn't be a major surprise that the Nasdaq has outperformed, as the AI trade has been strong throughout most of the year. That said, the tech-dominated index is still well off its all-time high despite the recent rebound after a November to forget. While these returns are very healthy by most investors' standards, 2025 has by no means been an easy year. The Liberation Day crash is clearly displayed in the above chart, and stocks have had to overcome obstacles like tariff concerns, geopolitical uncertainty, and AI bubble fears to get to where they are today.

Moving forward, will the market be able to replicate the performance of 2025? That's what we're here to discuss today. The S&P 500 currently sits at ~6,900 points, and my hope is to provide a well-reasoned target for the index by the end of this article. Let's jump in.

Current Market Fundamentals

Let's start with a look at where the market fundamentals stand now. In the above chart, the latest estimates for calendar year 2025 earnings growth are shown. With the exception of Energy, all sectors are expected to show positive annual growth. It is not a surprise that IT earnings are seeing the best momentum with the AI boom still much intact. At the end of the day, current estimates call for an 11.9% earnings increase for the S&P 500 annually from 2024 levels, and with just one month to go in 2025, I believe it is safe to say that these expectations will be quite accurate. For 2024, earnings growth for the S&P 500 was 9.22%, and so there are good signs that business momentum for 2025 as a whole is strong. Later in this article, we'll discuss how AI has the potential to keep accelerating earnings growth.

While business momentum for 2025 as a whole seems to be in quite a good place versus where we were in 2024, there are signs that there is some deceleration in the last quarter for this year. In the above chart, earnings growth estimates for Q4 specifically are shown, and the projected 7.7% increase would be well below the annual estimate and the 13.5% growth rate we saw in Q3. Therefore, when we zoom in, we do see some signs that the economy may indeed be slowing. Will this continue into 2026? Well, there's a bunch of factors in play that we'll highlight later.

So then, where is the market sitting in terms of valuation? In the above chart, the P/E ratio for the S&P 500 is shown. Of course, the multiple was inflated during late 2020 and early 2021 as the pandemic depressed earnings, but Fed policy boosted asset prices. Other than that major valuation spike, the current P/E of the S&P 500 at 29.21 represents a very elevated position. There are arguments both for overvaluation and against overvaluation. If you're looking at the deceleration of earnings momentum into Q4, then stocks may be deemed to be expensive. However, when looking at the annual acceleration from 2024 and potential market drivers as we head into 2026, this valuation may not be so expensive after all. So far, I have mostly just laid out the current fundamentals and estimates. Now, let's really get into where I think this market is headed.

The Elephant In The Room: AI

Recently, some investors have grown uneasy about the AI trade as bubble fears are surfacing. However, there are indications that these worries are not rooted in reality. In the above comparison provided by BlackRock (BLK), conditions of the Dot-Com bubble are contrasted with the AI boom we're seeing now. Clearly, there was much more froth back in the late 90s than there is today. While stock returns during the AI revolution do exceed earnings growth by a fair amount to show a good deal of optimism, valuations are nowhere near as inflated as back in 2000. Moving forward, this is a key development for my S&P 500 projection, as I believe that this comparison shows the likelihood of a severe market crash is very low at this point. Furthermore, I believe some investors' worries regarding AI ROI are also misplaced.

Back in August of this year, Morgan Stanley (MS) estimated that AI adoption could lead to up to $920 billion in annual net benefits for S&P 500 companies over time. While the realization of benefits may take years, this may ultimately result in market value creation in the range of $13 trillion to $16 trillion. Therefore, while the ROI of spending heavily on AI infrastructure may not be overly clear now, eventually, investments are likely to be a major driver of the global economy. How does this fit into my market projection?

Well, in annual terms we are already seeing acceleration in earnings growth for 2025, and so while it's hard to measure precisely, I would say there are good signs that AI is indeed powering the economy and S&P 500 companies to a great extent. With this key market driver still much intact, I would say that the slowdown of earnings growth into Q4 is most likely a temporary phenomenon rather than a trend of deceleration. With AI very likely to power earnings growth in the years to come, the elevated valuation we discussed earlier suddenly becomes much more reasonable.

Bears may say that Morgan Stanley's estimate is pure hopium. I believe the above chart proves that benefits from AI are very real. As you can see, gross margin, operating margin, and profit margin for the Nasdaq-100 are all on pace to hit record highs as profitability of companies continues to improve. It is no secret that AI adoption by enterprises targets business efficiency and cost control improvements, and so I would say there are very real signs that the benefits of AI are already getting realized to some degree. With effects more than likely to be more pronounced as the AI revolution continues into later this decade, there shouldn't be much question that Nasdaq-100 and S&P 500 earnings will benefit.

Earnings Growth To Accelerate

For 2026 as a whole, current estimates call for earnings growth of 14.5%. That would be a substantial acceleration from what we're seeing in 2025 and also shows that the earnings growth slowdown for Q4 is not expected to persist. Personally, I find this estimate to be a quite reasonable one. Given the clear AI drivers discussed above, I believe an acceleration of earnings growth for the S&P500 is indeed a likely outcome for 2026 and perhaps even beyond.

That's my view, but according to the data, how accurate will this estimate be? It turns out that FactSet did some research regarding this topic. In the past 25 years, one-year-in-advance bottom-up estimates for EPS have on average been overoptimistic by just 0.9% when excluding years with extraordinary circumstances such as the pandemic of 2020. As of now, the EPS of the S&P 500 for 2026 is expected to be $309.22, and so if we adjust for this average overoptimism, the EPS figure for 2026 is likely to be $306.34. Therefore, if there aren't any extraordinary circumstances in the coming calendar year, investors should expect optimism-corrected growth of 13.13%, assuming that EPS for 2025 will be at $270.79. That would still be an acceleration of growth from 2025, and so there are good reasons for optimism.

Wildcards & Risks For 2026

Tariff Risks Remain

In Q3 earnings calls, 238 S&P 500 companies mentioned tariffs. That's down 33% from Q2's 357, and so there are signs that businesses are putting this topic behind them. While it is indeed an encouraging sign that the tariff environment has stabilized somewhat, moving into 2026, I would say there is still some risk that tariffs will have notable effects on earnings and stock performance.

As of now, the Supreme Court still hasn't made a decision regarding the legality of President Trump's unprecedented executive action vis-a-vis tariffs. If the Supreme Court were to rule against Trump, there would be much uncertainty regarding steps that the administration would take and whether refunds of tariffs would occur. Therefore, while the buzz regarding this topic has seemingly decreased at the corporate level, I believe investors should still be prepared for some volatility resulting from this matter.

Inflation Still Above Target

Another factor that should still be top of mind is inflation. The talk about inflation isn't as hot as it was a few years ago when it neared 10%, but there are still significant concerns today. As you can see in the chart above, both overall U.S. inflation and the U.S. Core Inflation rate are currently at around 3%. While that is a major improvement from levels seen in 2022, note that this is still significantly above the Fed's long-term target of 2%.

Furthermore, tariffs and inflation are quite deeply intertwined. At this point, we still don't know if the full effect of tariffs on inflation has played out. With some companies having stockpiled products, there is still potential for lagged effects, and so moving into 2026, I would say that a further rebound in inflation is a notable risk to rate cuts and market performance.

The Fed & Rate Cut Uncertainty

Firstly, the Fed just delivered what many have called a hawkish rate cut yesterday (Dec 10th) of 25bps. That has brought the target rate down to the 350-375bps range. That's three straight cuts, but moving forward, the path to lower rates may not be so clear. The above chart from CME Group shows how many cuts the market is pricing in by the end of 2026. As of now, the most likely outcome, according to the market, is another 50bps of easing in the coming year, likely in the form of two more 25bps cuts.

I view this as a reasonable target, but the inflation concerns I outlined above are definitely still in play. If lagged tariff effects fuel an inflation rebound to significantly above 3%, it will make it difficult for the Fed to cut even two times in 2026, in my view. The market does see this risk, with some investors thinking just one cut or no cut at all. I do believe that 3 or more cuts in the next twelve months is a little ambitious given the risk of an inflation rebound, but political pressures may make it a reality.

The other uncertainty we have to deal with in 2026 is a new Fed chairman. Jerome Powell is set to step down in a few months, and Fed independence may be a concern moving forward. Just after the latest rate cut, President Trump expressed his view that the 25bps cut could have been "at least doubled." Regarding his nomination for a new Fed chair, Trump stated that he's "looking for somebody that will be honest with interest rates." This suggests to me that the President is aiming to select someone that's mainly dovish. While the market usually rewards rate cuts with gains, if cuts are due to political pressures and not driven by data, there is a risk that easing could do more harm than good.

My Target For The S&P500

Right now, the S&P 500 trades at a P/E ratio of around 29, as shown earlier. This is, of course, quite an elevated valuation relative to historic readings, and so there is understandably a bit of nervousness in some investors.

Firstly, I would say that AI drivers are able to justify this level of valuation. As discussed above, margins are expanding for companies, earnings growth is expected to accelerate in 2026 even when adjusting for overoptimism, and long-term AI benefits will be realized in the years to come. What may potentially lower the market's P/E ratio and valuation in the coming year are the risk factors that I laid out above. If tariffs or political pressure reignite inflation, investor panic could cause compression in the multiple to levels below where it is now. I would say that when incorporating these potential risks into my projection, a P/E of around 27 would be an appropriate base-case estimate for the S&P 500 by the end of next year.

This is a bit on the conservative side in my view, as this assumes that AI drivers don't become even more apparent and powerful over the next twelve months. Given the rapid development and innovation in the tech sector, long-term estimates of the benefits of AI may be revised higher by this time next year, and so expectations of valuation multiple contraction could be too pessimistic.

Nonetheless, assuming that the P/E ratio corrects to around 27 due to macro and political uncertainty and that the S&P 500 optimism-adjusted earnings estimate of $306.34 holds, I predict that the index will cross 8,200 points by the end of next year. This would be nearly a 20% gain for the S&P 500 from current levels, and so I believe it's safe to say that I'm very bullish on the market even in my base case.

Conclusion

Despite the overall good returns, 2025 hasn't been a particularly easy year for stocks. 2026 is not shaping up to be a straightforward year either, with there being considerable uncertainties on the macro and political sides of things. However, I believe the S&P 500 is still set to deliver another year of significant gains. The real driver to my bullish thesis is AI. In the near term (over the next year), AI is very likely to continue improving margins, profitability, and earnings for S&P 500 companies. Why do I think the index will at least sustain a P/E in the mid- to high 20s? Well, by the end of 2026, I expect investors to have a clearer sense of the long-term positive impacts of AI on businesses and the economy, and so optimism is unlikely to die down in my view. Therefore, even if some of the risks that I discussed materialize, I expect the S&P 500 to still hit my target of 8,200 points.

data.title: Dividend Kings: 3 Ideal Buys In 56 For December
data.description:
Foreword

As supplement to this article, please note that The Motley Fool, which sourced this latest list, and SureDividend both update their lists periodically. Kiplinger also featured this recent selection of Kings.

Note that two Kings failed to increase dividends last year and so departed from the list, Leggett and Platt (LEG), and 3M Co (MMM).

This October collection numbered 56 Kings when Automatic Data Processing (ADP) and RLI Corporation (RLI) joined the ranks back in February and MGE Energy was coronated in August.

Indeed, most of the King collection is too pricey to justify their skinny dividends. However, two of the top-ten, by yield, live up to the author’s ideal of offering annual dividends (from a $1K investment) exceeding their single share prices. Furthermore, this month, there are many more to watch.

In the current market adjustment, it is now possible for six, Altria Group (MO), Universal (UVV), Hormel Foods (HRL), Kenvue (KVUE), Canadian Utilities Ltd (CDUAF), and, United Bankshares [WV] (UBSI), to be fair-priced. Those fair priced six show their annual-yield (from $1,000 invested) meeting, or exceeding, their single-share prices.

The eighteen more to watch are: Target (TGT); Federal Realty (FRT); Stanley Black & Decker (SWK); Northwest Natural (NWN); Black Hills (BKH); Fortis Inc (FTS); H2O America (HTO); Stephan Co (SCL); Archer Daniels Midland (ADM); Sysco Corp (SYY); Coca-Cola (KO); Middlesex Water Co (MSEX); Colgate-Palmolive (CL); Gorman-Rupp (GRC); Commerce Bancshares (CBSH); Gorman-Rupp (GRC); RLI Corp; Tootsie-Roll (TR).

The chart above shows the Dollar Variant amount required for all 56 Kings to reach the tipping point. The six IDEAL show negative numbers. The eighteen to watch are all within $55 of goal.

As we are now nearing the six-year mark of the 2020 Ides of March dip, the time to snap-up five lingering top-yield dividend King dogs may be at hand... unless another big bearish drop in price looms ahead. (At which time your strategy would be to add to your position in any of those you then hold.)

To learn which (if any) of these Kings show “safer” dividends (with sufficient cash reserves to cover their payout) drop down to the Afterword at the tail of this article. On second thought, I’ll save you the trouble, there are three: MO; KVUE; UBSI.

Actionable Conclusions (1-10): Analysts Predict 19.89% To 93.08% Top-Ten Kingly Net Gains For December

Three of the ten top-Kings by yield were among the top-ten gainers for the coming year based on analyst 1-year target prices. (They are tinted gray in the chart below). Thus, this yield-based December 8 forecast (as graded by Brokers) was 30% accurate.

Estimated dividend returns from $1000 invested in each of these highest-yielding stocks and their aggregate one-year analyst median target-prices, as reported by YCharts, produced the following 2025-26 data points. (Note: target-prices from lone-analysts were not used.) Ten probable profit-generating trades projected to December, 2026 were:

Stepan was projected to net $930.82 based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 2 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 6% over the market as a whole.

Universal Corp was projected to net $545.90, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 2 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate is subject to risk/volatility 32% less than the market as a whole.

H2O America was projected to net $356.82, based on a median of target estimates from 4 analysts, plus dividends, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 50% less than the market as a whole.

Kimberly-Clark (KMB) was projected to net $293.29, based on the median of target price estimates from 17 analysts, plus dividends, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 75% less than the market as a whole.

National Fuel Gas (NFG) was projected to net $259.67 based on target price estimates from 5 analysts, plus annual dividend, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate is subject to risk/volatility 40% less than the market as a whole.

Procter & Gamble (PG) was projected to net $240.74, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 24 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate is subject to risk/volatility 62% less than the market as a whole.

California water Service Group (CWT) netted $233.12 based on a median target price estimate from 4 analysts, plus dividends, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 35% less than the market as a whole.

Sysco was projected to net $214.87, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 19 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 39% less than the market as a whole.

Stanley Black & Decker (SWK) was projected to net $214.71, based on the median of target price estimates from 19 analysts, plus the estimated annual dividend, less broker fees. The Beta number showed 20% over the market as a whole.

PPG industries (PPG) was projected to net $198.03, based on the median of target price estimates from 24 analysts, plus dividends, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 19% more than the market as a whole.

The average net gain in dividend and price was estimated to be 34.89% on $10k invested as $1k in each of these ten stocks. The average Beta ranking showed these estimates subject to risk/volatility 29% under the market as a whole.

The Dividend Dogs Rule

Stocks earned the "dog" moniker by exhibiting three traits: (1) paying reliable, repeating dividends, (2) their prices fell to where (3) yield (dividend/price) grew higher than their peers. Thus, the highest yielding stocks in any collection became known as "dogs." More precisely, these are, in fact, best called, "underdogs", even if they are "Kings."

36 Dividend Kings By 1 Year Total Returns

Sources: motleyfool.com/YCharts.com

This scale of annual returns provides a measure of market success. Note: only 36 positive annual returns from the Kings were detected. That left 20 reporting negative returns for the past year.

47 Dividend Kings By Broker Targets

This scale of broker-estimated upside (or downside) for stock prices provides a measure of market popularity. Note: no broker coverage or single broker coverage produced a zero score on the above scale. Negative broker ratings were ignored. These broker estimates can be seen as the emotional component (as opposed to the strictly monetary and objective dividend/price yield-driven report below). As noted above, these scores may also be regarded as contrarian.

56 Dividend Kings By Yield

56 Dividend Kings By Yield, Targets, & Returns Show None in Top 20 On All Three Charts

Actionable Conclusions (11-20): December Dogs Of The Dividend Kings

Top ten King Dogs selected 12/8/25 by yield represented four of eleven Morningstar sectors.

The first of six consumer staples representatives took first place, Altria Group Inc (MO) [1]. Others placed second through sixth: Universal Corp [2], Kenvue [3], Kimberly-Clark [4] Hormel Foods [5], and Target [6].

A single real estate member was eighth, Federal Realty [7]. Thereafter, one industrials representative placed eighth, Stanley Black & Decker [8].

Finally, two utilities placed ninth and tenth, Canadian Utilities [9], and Northwest Natural Holding [10] to complete these December top-ten Kings, by yield.

Dividend Vs. Price Results

The graph above shows relative strengths of the top ten November Kings by dividend and price as of market close 11/6/2025.

This month five of the top-ten Kings by yield show a less than ideal status because the prices of those six exceed the projected annual dividends from $1k invested in each.

As noted above, a dividend dogcatcher IDEAL priority is to select stocks whose dividends from $1K invested are greater than their single share price. That condition was reached by just five of the highest yield Kings.

Altria, Universal, Hormel, Kenvue, and Canadian Utilities, in the top ten, were IDEAL. Those five lived up to the dogcatcher ideal of annual dividends from $1K invested exceeding single share prices.

56 Dividend King Costs per Dividend Dollar as of 11/6/25

The above chart reveals 56 King Dividend dogs subjected to the same test: divide the price of each stock by the annual dividend. Thus is calculated the cost per dividend dollar for each stock.

The least expensive dog of the November Kings was Altria at $13.51 per dividend dollar and the most expensive is S&P Global at $127.60 per dividend dollar.

Actionable Conclusion (21): Twenty of Fifty-Six Kings Show Negative Free Cash Flow Margins

A negative gap between dividend yield and free cash flow yield defines a cash-poor stock. That is, their dividend payout exceeds their cash on hand to pay dividends.Twenty dividend Kings so infected are: Kimberly-Clark, Hormel Foods, Federal Realty, Stanley Black & Decker, Canadian Utilities, Northwest Natural, PepsiCo, Black Hills, Fortis, H2O America, Consolidated Edison, Stephan, Genuine Parts, Coca-Cola, American States Water, California Water, National Fuel Gas, MGE Energy, ABM Industries, and Nucor. These are considered unsafe to buy.

Remember this dogcatcher yield-based stock-picking strategy is contrarian. That means rooting for (buying) the underdog is productive when you don't already own those stocks. If you do hold those stocks, then you must look for opportune pull-backs in price to add to your position to best improve your dividend yield. Plenty of pull-back opportunities appear to be ahead. Dread the Fed, deportation, uncertainty, deals, wars, detention and tariffs, too!

Price Drops or Dividend Increases Could Get All Ten Top-Yield Kings Back to "Fair Price" Rates For Investors

The charts above retain the current dividend amount and adjust share price to produce a yield (from $1K invested) to equal or exceed the single share price of each stock. As the top illustration shows, five in the top ten are already ideally priced. Beside those, already in the ideal zone, five more top-yield stocks are within $40 of getting there.

The alternative, of course, could be that these companies raise their dividends or declare stock splits to achieve fair pricing. That may be too much to ask in these highly disrupted, inflationary, recessionary, uncertain, yet cash-rich, and volatile times. Mr. Market is quite effective at moving prices up or down to appropriate amounts, just watch and buy when your targeted stock price moves into a sweet spot.

Actionable Conclusions: (21-31) Top Ten Kings Showed 17.88% To 90.54% Upsides Into December 2026; (31) Two Down-siders Lagged -0.59% to 7.15%

To quantify top-yield rankings, analyst median price-target estimates made a "market sentiment" gauge of upside potential. Added to the simple high-yield metrics, analyst median price-target-estimates became another tool to dig-out bargains.

Analysts Estimated A 10.38% Advantage For 5 Highest Yield, Lowest Priced, of Top-Ten Dividend Kings By December, 2026

Ten top Kings were culled by yield for their monthly update. Yield (dividend/price) results verified by YCharts did the ranking.

As noted above, top ten Kings selected 11/6/25 showing the highest dividend yields represented five of eleven Morningstar sectors.

Actionable Conclusions: Analysts Estimated 5 Lowest-Priced Of Ten Highest-Yield Dividend Kings (32) Delivering 15.00% Vs. (33) 18.88% Net Gains by All Ten by November, 2026

$5000 invested as $1k in each of the five lowest-priced stocks in the top ten Dividend Kings kennel by yield were predicted by analyst 1-year targets to deliver 10.38% more gain than $5,000 invested as $.5k in all ten. The sixth lowest-priced top-yield King stock, Universal, was projected to deliver the best net gain of 54.59%.

The five lowest-priced top-yield dividend Kings as of December 8 were: Kenvue; Hormel Foods; Canadian Utilities; Northwest Natural Holding; Universal, with prices ranging from $16.76 to $101.85

Five higher-priced top-yield Dividend Kings by the December 8 accounting were: Altria; Stanley Black & Decker; Target; Federal Realty; Kimberly-Clark, whose prices ranged from $58.11 to $101.85.

This distinction between five low-priced dividend dogs and the general field of ten reflected Michael B. O'Higgins' "basic method" for beating the Dow. The scale of projected gains based on analyst targets added a unique element of "market sentiment" gauging upside potential. It provided a here-and-now equivalent of waiting a year to find out what might happen in the market. Caution is advised, however, since analysts are historically only 15% to 85% accurate on the direction of change and just 0% to 15% accurate on the degree of change.

Afterword

24 ‘Safer' December Dividend Kings

Early December, 2025, 24 of 56 Dividend Kings were tagged "safer" for dividends by showing positive one-year returns and free-cash-flow-yields greater-than dividend-yields. Furthermore, YCharts 12/8/25 data showed three were fair-priced.

Dividend King ‘Safer’ Dividend Vs. Price Data Show “Fair Price” Results For Investors

The chart at the top above reveals three dividends from $1K invested in our top ten 'safer' dividend Kings conform to the dogcatcher standard of being equal to (or greater than) the price of a single share. They are: Altria, Kenvue, and United Bankshares.

The middle chart shows the recent dividend amount and the bottom chart adjusts that share price to produce a yield (from $1K invested) equal to or greater than the single share price of each stock; a so-called, “fair price.”

A market correction of 69% could put all ten share prices of these “safer” survivors above or equal to their dividend payouts from $1k invested.

There are, of course, other ways for dividends from $1K invested to exceed the price of a single share of stock. That would be for the boards of directors these companies to raise dividends to the dogcatcher ideal level. to Split shares. That’s a lot to ask with all the corporate funds doing so much “good” elsewhere.

Stay tuned for future Dividend Dogcatcher King updates, out here, in public, away from the pay-walled Seeking Alpha Investing Group site (formerly known as the Marketplace).

Footnotes

Twenty Three “Safer” Dividend Kings Distilled to Three Ideal Dogcatcher Royals

A Descending List of Dividend King Longevity

The net gain estimates mentioned above did not factor-in any foreign or domestic tax problems resulting from distributions. Consult your tax advisor regarding the source and consequences of "dividends" from any investment.

Stocks listed above were suggested only as possible starting points for your "Safer" Kingly dividend stock research process. These were not recommendations.

Disclaimer: This article is for informational and educational purposes only and should not be construed to constitute investment advice. Nothing contained herein shall constitute a solicitation, recommendation or endorsement to buy or sell any security. Prices and returns on equities in this article except as noted are listed without consideration of fees, commissions, taxes, penalties, or interest payable due to purchasing, holding, or selling same.

Graphs and charts were compiled by Rydlun & Co., LLC from data derived from YCharts; finance.yahoo.com; analyst mean target price by YCharts. Open source dog art from dividenddogcatcher.com.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Keep It Simple With Bonds And ETFs
data.description:
Listen here or on the go via Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Rob Isbitts from Sungarden Investors Club on the S&P 500 sucking up all the air in the room and the top things he's thinking about (0:50). Building a bond ladder (3:10). Playing ETFs on offense and defense (9:00). The last few weeks have seen typical market behavior (26:45).

Transcript

Rena Sherbill: Rob Isbitts from Sungarden Investment Publishing and his Investing Group, Sungarden Investors Club. Welcome back to Investing Experts. Great to have you back on.

Rob Isbitts: Hey, it's great to be here again, Rena. And this actually comes at a pretty opportune time. I just wanted to say I just hit a pretty significant milestone at Seeking Alpha, 10,000 followers.

It's been about three years on the platform and I just wanted to thank everybody at Seeking Alpha and the audience. I can sometimes be an acquired taste, not for everybody, but the mission was to try to use this part of my semi-retired life to get to a wider audience and I think that round number is pretty good validation.

Rena Sherbill: Congrats, that's awesome. 10,000 followers. There's nothing to sneeze at.

So talk to us. How are you looking at these markets? You were on a few weeks ago with the next gen investors, Julia Ostia and Kenio Fontes, and Jack Bowman. And so you were sharing some of your thoughts then, but if you could synthesize it into your top three things that you're thinking about.

Rob Isbitts: I would start with the idea that what they call the passive bid or index investing has really become the 800 pound gorilla for this market. And it's frankly made it so that it is really difficult, I think, to make a case that investing has to be nearly as complex as it used to be. It doesn't mean that we don't need to do deep research. We do.

But and I think the other two things are kind of related to that. So it's the indexation of the markets, the fact that so much money is chasing the same trade, if you will, but it's a long term trade for a lot of people. And that's the S&P 500 (SP500). It's kind of sucking up all the air in the room. And I think the second thing would be that I believe that risk management is even more important because of that going into 2020.

2026 because it's a very deceiving market, being very crowded at the top with stocks. And the third thing has nothing to do with the stock market, even though it does influence the stock market. And I think it's going to be a big influencer in 2026.

And that is the bond market, specifically the treasury yield curve. We've gotten very used to years of zero interest rates going back to the global financial crisis in 08, 09, but since that time, rates were really suppressed for a while. 2022, they popped up, they've stayed up. And where has that led me in my own portfolio?

As I've written in some I think fairly popular articles, that's got a lot of folks off of the bench, if you will. The subject was not the stock market or necessarily trading or even long-term investing fundamental tactics. It was the simplicity of building a bond ladder using zero coupon treasuries, which I've done. And I talked pretty liberally about what I did, why I've done it.

I'll continue to do that because of the comments have just been through the moon. Great in terms of making me realize that there's a whole generation of investors of different ages who've never had a care about bonds before and never saw the need for them.

And there's something about the simplicity of saying, you know what? I've got X amount of money coming due every year, the years I choose, the dates I choose, and, and if rates and inflation go up, I can hedge it. And it's what I call treasuries plus affectionately. And, so it's become a big sleep well at night thing for me personally and look this is what I write about it Seeking Alpha what I'm doing personally.

Rena Sherbill: We had Michael Kramer on talking about the bond market and he was talking about how the startling thing about the rates going down to your point is that he feels like not everybody is, or not enough people are talking about the fact that the 10 year rate has not gone down with it, along with the 30 year rate.

What is your thought on the bond market as it pertains to the interest rate conversation? And then also, is your, as you talk about recommending or encouraging investors to think about bonds, what does that mean exactly?

Rob Isbitts: I'm a big fan of Michael's work and I'll take it from the different angle. He's talking about rates have not gone down and I'm looking at where they've come from. This is the highest rate that I believe we've seen for at least the 10 to 30 year part of the treasury curve, maybe five or seven years out as well.

These are the highest rates we've seen just about since probably 20 years ago and it's not also not lost on me the fact that rates had a similar looking rise peak stagnation period event if you will around the time of the dot-com bubble, which I think this environment reminds me of a lot even though it's not the same it rhymes like crazy so I look at it and I say, there's a limited amount of time, I think, for people to really lock in.

My own ladder starts at five years, goes about 20 years, going to last me until I'm 80 years old and I'll work on 80 to 90 eventually. But there's something about that that I just don't think people get because when it comes to, I mean, there's two ways you can take it.

I look at a bond ladder and the bond market in general as a level of certainty. Now, we have to be careful because nothing in investing is certain. But to the extent that T-bills may be considered the risk-free asset, right? Always have been, I think, or for a long time, many, many decades.

But the bond market and the treasury market, yes, there's always the possibility, maybe a little bit more now than ever, a sneaking suspicion that, you know, investors are going to lose their confidence in the US Congress in particular to be able to regulate their debt and rein it in.

Nobody wants to cut spending and this could be a long-term problem. know, Japan's had their own issues with that over a few decades. That didn't turn out well either, aging population and all that.

So there's some similarities, but I think the bottom line and why it leads me to this now say, okay, after the stock market run, at least at the top, not just this year, but for the last decade or so, where can you go to get, let's call it a four and a half percent give or take return where you are locking in nominal dollar amounts that you're going to receive almost like a second social security payment or pension, but that you don't put all your money in it.

There's stock portfolio, ETFs, options, whatever, and other ways to hedge interest rates. But if rates were to plummet, which is entirely possible, people may forget about the U.S. debt for a minute and just go flight to quality as has happened before. I mean, I'm kind of open to anything with this. Now that it's structured, but if you've got four and a half percent, give or take, you know, out several years, rates go up, you can hedge.

Rates go down, part of the expression, but investors will crush it in a way that they probably never even seen from things like dividend stocks and certainly lower quality bonds. I'm, you know, I think everything has risk to it.

But I'll take the risk that the U S government won't be able to pay me over time. it may be with inflated dollars. And like I said, again, you can hedge that and I've lot of ways to hedge it. can discuss that, but that, that really to me is like, why not anchor this, especially when, some of the, think most noted tenured, investors are talking about a possibility of somewhere between a lost decade and very modest positive returns for the equity market.

And I'm not going to sit here and try to predict the future. I just know there's a very high risk that equities are not going to make huge strides for the next five years the way they have the last five.

Rena Sherbill: So you have a series of ETFs that allows you to play offense. And then the hedge on the other side is the defensive ETFs. Do you want to go through that model portfolio and then maybe share with investors why those ETFs specifically and why the individual allotment or why the respective allotment per ETF or per theme?

Rob Isbitts: Sure, sure. And if I can, know, going back to kind of the order in which we spoke about this. I think that the subject matter here is simplicity. The idea that I think a lot of investors, especially self-directed types, are really overthinking this. And it's not a flaw of theirs. It's maybe lack of recognition that the markets have become so binary. mean, the stock market is almost like one risk on risk off trade.

I've heard the market with all the speculation referred to as a casino, but with better lighting. And to me, that means that we have to start with, let's spend most of our effort trying to do more with fewer items. So it starts with simplicity. The bonds are kind of a side saddle part of that for those who believe like I do that it'd be nice to have a fixed return that can be hedged or can profit from lower rates and kind of gives you some flexibility there. bonds are almost like the somewhat static anchor piece.

The rest of it is, okay, what are you going to do with the rest of the money? It's not all in a bond ladder like I have, even though it is the biggest part of my portfolio. But look, I love ETFs. I used to love stock picking, but now I think it's not useless, but it's a lot less useful than it used to be because of algorithms, indexation, crowded trade, wherever you want to call it. So here's what I did for the folks at the Sungarden Investors Club.

I referred to this now as the weekly ROAR and we talked about ROAR a little bit before. It's my reward opportunity and risk proprietary indicator, which basically says anything go up at any time for any reason. but how much risk of major loss are you taking on? I always look at it as, as how much risk am I taking once the coast is clear.

Now I can try to invest and make as much as I can, but risk management is first and the whole roar idea started about four years ago. I started writing about this and the simple idea was what you see in the top two here.

Rena Sherbill: And what are those top two just for those listening?

Rob Isbitts: Yeah, sure, it's the S&P 500 ETF (SPY) and whatever your favorite short-term T bill, the ETF is I use is (BIL) cause it's an easy ticker. Some people use (SGOV), et cetera. So they're interchangeable.

There's SPY at the top and BIL for the defense. I think most investors have learned how to play offense. Very few have learned how to play defense alongside it.

And there's a lot of sports analogies that I will skip over for today, but I've written about a lot of them. It starts with the idea that look, the way markets have evolved now, T-bill's being pretty competitive with yield and the S &P 500 again, soaking up all the air in the room to the point where sure you can beat it and stock picking and all that, but there are so many investors that I think are either learning or relearning things like risk management, how to incorporate things other than stocks.

And all I keep looking at in every study I do is that a big portion of the stock market has gone kind of nowhere for several years. So we might as well start at the beginning, as they say, with a very straightforward SPY and BIL portfolio. And for the last four years, I have run just that with one of my accounts.

And it's not only one of my best performing accounts personally, it's beaten a very long list of things that you wouldn't think it has any right to beat. Basically anything all the way up to let's say a 60/40 and beyond, more so than a lot of dividend ETFs, more so than the (JEPI)s of the world, coming in at about an 8% return, but with a standard deviation under three, for those who understand standard deviation, that is what I call getting a lot of bang for your buck or managing risk to almost to a fault and then trying to make as much as you can.

And so all I was able to do to generate that, which I've published about a lot and I will some more is SPY/BIL. That's all I used. No stocks, no other ETFs, SPY/BIL and my ROAR score, which every Tuesday I publish for subscribers and I simply say, this is how much is in SPY and this is how much is in BIL because the ROAR score, which is a number from zero to a hundred hundreds maximum risk willing to take because the market is easy, which almost never happens and zero, which it's been a little bit, in, some past crises, zero basically means I don't want any stock market related risk.

So I start with how risky is the stock market, not for little short-term pullbacks, but major loss. And the number has been fairly low for much of this year. And even though the S and P has done pretty well, the average stock, I think it's up maybe 4% in 13 months. The (RSP) is the ETF. So what do I do with this group? I start with offense and defense. Just let's put labels on it like that.

And SPY/BIL for a newer investor or somebody who's newer to ETFs or somebody who's just looking for, let's say a ballast in their portfolio. I've got two ballasts here. I've got SPY/BIL, two ETF portfolio run by the Roar score. And I've got the aforementioned bond ladder.

But the next set of questions I would get from people is, well, what about this ETF, that ETF? So many market segments move in sync. If the big drug stocks move, there's a good chance healthcare is moving, but not every time and not during earnings, but in broad strokes, again, trying to keep it simple.

There's just way too much. I'm going to call it correlation nation. The US stock market has become correlation nation. Things are too highly correlated to want to go way outside the box.

It doesn't mean that every day there aren't great stocks to pick, but the problem is with the algorithms and the indexation being these sort of indifferent, somewhat passive forces that don't care where they're buying and selling. We've talked about this before. With so much of the money being run that way, I think it's something like 70 to 90 % of stock market trading activity every day doesn't care what is buying or selling. It's doing it because of some rule, because it has to fill an index portfolio.

And to me, if we don't adapt as investors to that modern reality, then we are just tempting fate. So what I did with Spy Bill is I said, okay, let me expand the list. Let me expand the offense and the defense from one choice each for the simplest yet very effective portfolio over the last four years, two, six of each. I could go to a hundred of each, but the problem would be, and I know this from being a lifelong chartist, I can chart them all week after week and so many of them look the same.

So I'm happy for subscribers to comment on anything they want to talk about in our live meetings and et cetera. So, so on the offense, it starts with SPY, but in reality, one of the other little, what do they call it, hacks, like a stock market hack?

Beyond SPY, there's the Qs, (QQQ), NASDAQ 100, and the Dow (DIA). Because as time has gone on, a lot more money has gone into the Qs from indexing than the Dow. The Qs have started to look like the SPY. The Dow correlates highly, but sometimes it goes its own way.

It's almost like it's the defensive version of large cap stocks. So what I will sometimes do is say, okay, spies possibility, but if you want to add a little bit more nuance to it, you can allocate between Qs and DIA. If you want to get a step further and start to increase, let's say the depth of market cap you look at, there's RSP, which is the equal weighted S and P 500, which by the way is now at a historic underperformance of SPY. Let me repeat that.

SPY, the S and P 500, all 500 stocks, RSP is the S and P 500, all 500 stocks. If you look at a list of the two, unless there's some time error in terms of they're on the wrong dates. You see the same 500 stocks. However, SPY is crowded at the top with something like 20 stocks occupying nearly half the index.

What about the other 480? Well, you capture those in RRSP because every stock is the same. Every stock is one fifth of 1% as opposed to Nvidia (NVDA), which is like 8%. So it's the same index, it's the same stocks, but the weighting is so different.

And that is one of the things that I try to use this as a teaching tool. People can then take it and do what they like because they're self-directed investors. Even those first four, SPY is what everybody knows. Qs and DIA to some extent are complements to each other, a little more of one, a little less of the other. It's not either or. It's not timing strategy.

And RSP and SPY are also kind of different ends of the same spectrum. Same stocks, completely different weighting. And RSP is underperforming mightily, which also has bled into the mid cap and the small cap underperformance. I don't know if it will change, but if it does, I believe it will show up in my chart work. And that's what I keep people on the pulse of every week.

So that's the offense. And I throw in oil and Bitcoin, which I'm not the biggest fan of, but hey, I'm a fan of making money and there's enough volatility there to create opportunity. So that's the offensive set. Any questions on that? Otherwise we can run through the defense.

Rena Sherbill: Let's hit the defense.

Rob Isbitts: All right, great. You've got BIL on the defense. Well, what else can you do that is defensive? I've talked about it before. You have a laddered bond portfolio. Well, mine's a zero coupon ladder, but there is a security I've used a lot tactically, symbol's (GOVI), and it is one to 30 year treasury is about 3.3 % weighted into each one of them. And so it is a ladder now. Yeah, sure. The bonds mature and all that, but it's going to be different. It's not your money that matures in the amount you want on a specific date in time.

So it's ladder format but it's not the same as a specific ladder that I teach people to build themselves like the one I built. But GOVI at least gets you beyond the T-bills. Now you've got sort of a little taste of everything. It probably averages to about a seven to 10, 12 year fixed income security issued by US government.

Then you've got gold (GLD), which I consider it defensive. I think it's kind of amorphous, but I put it in the gold in that category. So the offense has oil (OILK) and Bitcoin (IBIT) and the defense has gold. And that's how I cover sort of commodities and crypto in this sort of next level, basic ETF basket to allocate among.

And then there's three more. And they all have to do with hedging or profiting from down markets. One, and this is at least a threat, don't think it's going to go this way, but it might, if the global bond market loses, if the US loses credibility in the eyes of the global bond market, there may be no better way to take advantage of spiking inflation and spiking US long-term bond yields than ticker (TBF), there are double and triple lever versions of it as well.

People say to me, but if rates go up, your bond portfolio is screwed. Completely wrong. That was the case maybe 20, 25 years ago when we didn't really have as many tools. This is my whole thing, You know, the tool set has expanded greatly and that's why we have to kind of meet that complexity without overdoing it by owning 200 stocks in our portfolio, all of which are going to move together.

So TBF, all it does is it shorts the long treasury. In 2022, if you look back when rates were going up, up, up, so was TBF. And so to me, it's an easy way to hedge.

My ladder is also an easy way to hedge even the GOVI portfolio. So teaching people how to these basic ETF tools together and the last two need an equity inverse and technically it could be anyone I mean I teach people that I go beyond this (RWM) is inverse small caps the Russell 2000 that the IWM is based on RWM is the opposite of that there are levered versions, but this isn't one just Negative one times.

So when small caps go down this goes up the Russell 2000, as I think I've heard your other guests say, 40% of the companies are only surviving till their next debt refinancing.

And so to me, small caps are always sort of the punching bag and the easy way to go to say if the market in general is going to fall, the small caps are going to get it much worse. And lastly, I have kind of a, what I call a wild card spot right now. What's in there? It's one security that I say, you know what? You're probably never going to own five per say even five percent of this thing Maybe at most five percent of your total ETF portfolio

And so you want it to be something that is going to be tactical. You're not going to use it for very long. But it could be a huge difference maker either in a straight-up market or a straight down market blowout kind of like we had last April and 2020 and all that flash crash, if you will.

And right now what's sitting in the wildcard spot is (UVIX). is twice the movement of the VIX, the volatility index. Suffice it to say, if the VIX is sitting at 15 to 20 and it doesn't move much, UVIX will just keep losing value. But if I have a small position, I'm okay. If you get another April, you will see something like UViX double the VIX and the VIX goes from 15 to 20 range where it's been sitting in the last several years to 30, 40, maybe 60 or even 80.

Well, I have personally owned UVIX and others like it when these things have gone up like five, 600 % in a matter of a couple of months. And then just as quickly they can give it all back. So for those who don't want to get maybe as wonky as I tend to sometimes with options and colors and things like that, the volatility ETFs are about the best thing I have found as an option surrogate.

So UVIX would be almost like instead of a put option, you want to own something in ETF, but it is going to waste away in value the way an option does. If things don't go well as that (SVIX) is on the opposite side of that. believe that's an unlevered, but there's a basket of these and I try to write about them. certainly cover them in the Investors Club.

Rena Sherbill: What are things of value that investors could keep in mind with all the noise coming at them?

Rob Isbitts: As I see it, what's happening the last few weeks in the markets, let's say late November into early December here is just, it's so typical of how markets work. the Fed's coming pals gonna speak. The market's gonna move it's gonna move the other way then it's gonna move the way it really wants to move and I'll capture a lot of this in the charts.

But in the same way that you have let's say earnings for Nvidia (NVDA) or the other mag 7 everything just stops and so I'm sitting here as we're recording this I'm a few hours from my next weekly live session with with the group and I put out my weekly ROAR to the group subscribers on Tuesdays. And I'm sitting here saying, I think I want to take a 24 to 48 hour hold because Tuesday is not an action day when Wednesday is a Fed meeting because nothing is moving. Everything flattens out. It gets like so quiet.

You can hear a pin drop and the NASDAQ is moving in like a few point range. Okay. Nothing interesting is happening. So what do you do about that? What do you do about the fact that the markets have changed?

Well, first of all, there's no reason to push and jump the gun. It's another reason why having a portfolio that is very sort of simplified and static, and then you can trade around it as much as you like, because that's what I do. I a lot of trading, but it's with smaller amounts of money. you know, so honestly, mean, what's the Fed going to do?

Doesn't really matter to me what the market's going to do in reaction to that. That matters to me, but I'm not going to try to guess it. I gave up trying to become a professional guesser on time ago, but I will tell you what I'm focused on. And what I'm focused on is the aforementioned 10 to 30 year part of the treasury curve, because one of two things is likely to happen. Maybe not this week or even this month, or even this year, cause we're going into January of 26.

But I would guess that sometime within the first quarter of next year, the bond market is going to tell the stock market what to do because it usually does when the rubber meets the road, as they say. One of two things is going to happen. And there may be some things in between, but these are the two likely candidates. Okay. These are two major parties, if you will.

The long bond 10 to 30 year is going to react to the fact that they do not think that the U.S. is getting its fiscal house in order. And I'll leave all the politics out of it, but let's face it, it's not the usual U.S. survey, okay? So they may rebel, okay? I think Eddie Ardeni calls it the bond vigilantes. And they will push the rates up and they will repeat trade the money the Japanese yen carry trade, I'm sure we won't get into today, but that has to play in it too.

So that's one possibility and that may send rates up. Well, it's a nice thing that I have TBF there because that is the rate rise crusher. I can put a lot of money into that and be profitable even in a down bond market. If long rates go up, that could hurt the stock market unless the market is so convinced is because our growth is going to be great.

Unemployment is under control. I don't think it is and inflation is under control long-term and I think it sort of is but not enough and then the other thing that can happen of course is that as the president of United States and his followers would like is to get those rates way back down get them all the way to zero and I'm sitting back here and saying I don't care about the politics I don't really even care what happens, I just want something that has a trend, a trend that is going to last more than let's say a couple of weeks for a tactical trade.

And if it turns out that instead of the aforementioned spike higher in long-term rates, which would be a big issue for the treasury issuance, not to mention a lot of those small cap companies barring at higher rates, a lot of them may go kaput. If rates go the other way and they really start coming down hard, all of a sudden, well, there's the GOVI in there.

So the GOVI is one tool for that. It probably helps the stock market and got plenty of ammunition there. Probably will rekindle the hodlers of Bitcoin ilk. I've got a little piece of everything. It's a macro set here and you can get as micro as you want, but a lot of stuff ought to work. And if that's the case then again, one more time, going back to the bond ladder. remember what happened the last time bonds go down.

There's something called duration and you can pretty much assume that if a bond is in the, let's say, bond ladder is in the 10, 20, even 30 year maturity range, that if the 10 year rate goes down by even 1% that bond ladder might appreciate certainly by double digits might be 15, even 17%.

And so what if happens if rates go down two or 3%, Trump gets what he wants. Your rates are extremely low again. I don't know what that will do for mortgage rates. If those are still high. But if those come down too, and the whole yield curve comes down, you're talking about positive returns from a plain old bond portfolio. Whose worst case scenario is that you'll get paid a fixed amount of money that you determine now on a date you determine now years from now.

So to me, that kind of wraps it all in together and why sure, what the Fed does is important, but it's really more important what the market does in reaction to it. And I will give you a very similar argument for just about any market event. Cause at the end of the day, I'm just a chartist. I'm not into the narratives. I'm just into what actually happens on the playing field. And that's what the charts are.

Rena Sherbill: Well, appreciate it, Rob. Appreciate the conversation. Again, you write under Sungarden Investment Publishing. Your Investing Group on Seeking Alpha is called Sungarden Investors Club. Happy for you to share where else you share your analysis and thoughts and anything else you want to leave listeners with for this year and as we head into a new one.

Rob Isbitts: I'm just about to write my 2026 preview, but thank you for asking. I mean, I do a lot of writing a barchart. I get a lot of my chart work from there too. I used that thing for a long time and now I write five articles, sometimes even 10 articles a week for them. A lot of option collar stuff. It's a nice complement frankly to the, let's call it the format and the mantra at Seeking Alpha. And we reopened our substack. It's called ETF yourself, nothing personal, okay? it's a different type of service, a little lighter, highlights some of this sort of macro stuff here. And I've been researching ETFs since 1993 because that's when they first had ETFs. So I'm not hard to find.

In closing, we didn't really mention AI here, but one of the great things about AI, I think, is that if you have done a lot of content work, I'll say this to all of my Seeking Alpha writing peers. If you've done a lot of work, whether it's at Seeking Alpha or otherwise, just put your name in and say, tell me about Rob Isbitts AI or tell me about Sungarden and whatever. And it will tell you all about the ROAR score and it will even ask you questions that you might want to ask about the ROAR score and about Sungarden and about Rob's approach and blah, blah, blah. So I'm saying, AI is a great companion for this also, nobody has the excuse anymore that, I couldn't find you.

Rena Sherbill: Yes, nobody has that excuse anymore. Very few people get to live under a rock for real. And AI is our friend for anybody who wants to avail themselves. It can be used for our good. All right, Rob, appreciate the conversation.

Rob Isbitts: Haha, thank you so much, Rena, I really enjoyed it as usual.

data.title: The State Of REITs: December 2025 Edition
data.description:
REIT Performance

After a couple of tough months, the REIT sector rebounded in November with a +1.02% return. This narrows the year-to-date negative total return to -2.55% for the average REIT. REITs outperformed the broader market in November with average returns exceeding that of the Dow Jones Industrial Average (+0.5%), S&P 500 (+0.2%) and NASDAQ (-1.4%). The market cap weighted Vanguard Real Estate ETF (VNQ) more than doubled the return of the average REIT in November (+2.42% vs. +1.02%) and has handily outperformed year-to-date (+5.63% vs. -2.55%). The spread between the 2026 FFO multiples of large cap REITs (16.2x) and small cap REITs (12.8x) widened in November as multiples expanded 0.2 turns for large caps and 0.1 turns for small caps. Investors currently need to pay an average of 26.6% more for each dollar of FFO from large cap REITs relative to small cap REITs.
